title: Parental psychological problems were associated with higher screen time and the use of mature-rated media in children.
abstract: AIM: Parents' psychological problems may affect children's screen time, but research has been scarce. We examined the association between parental psychological problems and children's screen media behaviours in a nationally representative sample. METHODS: The participants were from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development study, recruited by probability sampling from the USA population. Children reported their use of TV, videos, video games, social media and mature-rated media. The parents (85% mothers) reported psychological problems using the Adult Self-Report questionnaire. RESULTS: In 10,650 children (5112Â girls, 5538 boys) aged 9.9Â Â±Â 0.6Â years, the presence of parental psychological problems was associated with children spending more daily time on screen media and with meeting the recommendation of â¤2 daily hours less often than children whose parents did not have psychological problems. Parental psychological problems were associated with children's TV watching, video watching and gaming but not with using social media. Parental internalising problems were associated with children watching mature-rated movies (odds ratio [OR]Â =Â 1.14, 95% confidence interval [CI]Â =Â 1.00, 1.30) and playing mature-rated games (ORÂ =Â 1.27, 95% CIÂ =Â 1.11, 1.45). CONCLUSION: Presence of parental psychological problems is associated with higher screen time and use of mature-rated media in children. This cross-sectional study was not able to examine causal associations.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study baseline data (2016-2018), N=10,650 children aged 9.9±0.6 years

FT_IC2.evidence: Paper measures general screen time categories (TV, videos, video games, social media, mature-rated media) but does NOT measure a SPECIFIC smartphone or social media variable as the primary exposure. Measures generic screen time across modalities without phone-specific or SM-specific metrics. Generic total screen time composite without phone/SM breakdown.

FT_IC3.evidence: Screen time is dependent variable, not independent variable. Parental psychological problems are predictors. No smartphone/SM variable is linked to a non-SM outcome as required by FT-IC3.

FT_IC4.evidence: FT-IC2 failure prevents scoring. Screen time is outcome, not exposure variable.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical cross-sectional analysis with inferential statistics (multilevel regression, logistic regression)

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article (Acta Paediatrica, 2022); no evidence of duplicate

C1.FT_EC7_reason: Does not reach FT-EC7 evaluation. Paper fails FT-IC2 (no specific phone/SM variable) and FT-IC3 (phone/SM as DV, parental psychology as IV, no phone/SM→non-SM outcome). Fails inclusion criteria before FT-EC7 is evaluated.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study data, N=10,650 children (5,112 girls, 5,538 boys) aged 9.9±0.6 years, cross-sectional baseline assessment

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures specific screen media variables: TV watching, video watching (YouTube), online games, social media (Twitter, Instagram), mature-rated video games, mature-rated movies. Operationalized via 7-point Likert scale for daily usage hours.

FT_IC3.evidence: Reports multilevel general linear models (for continuous screen time in minutes/day) and multilevel logistic regression models (for meeting vs not meeting ≤2h/day recommendation). Tests associations between parental psychological problems (predictor) and children's screen time/media behaviors (outcomes).

FT_IC4.evidence: Reports OR, 95% CI, and regression coefficients: 'Parental internalising problems were associated with children watching mature-rated movies (OR=1.14, 95% CI=1.00, 1.30) and playing mature-rated games (OR=1.27, 95% CI=1.11, 1.45)'

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical cross-sectional analysis of ABCD baseline data examining associations between parental psychological problems and children's screen media behaviors

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in Acta Paediatrica, 2022

C2.FT_EC7_reason: FT_EC7 does NOT apply. Fails criterion (c): Study is CROSS-SECTIONAL (not longitudinal/prospective). Additionally, fails criterion (b): Parental psychological problems include psychopathology/clinical dimensions (internalizing, externalizing tendencies), not ONLY pure demographics (SES, race, sex, age, etc.). Per FT-EC7 rule, includes individual psychological/clinical predictor → EC7 does not apply; keep INCLUDE.

title: Sexual and gender minority identity, peer victimization, and suicidality in adolescents: AÂ mediation study using the ABCD Study.
journal: Journal of child psychology and psychiatry, and allied disciplines
abstract: BACKGROUND: Sexual and gender minority (SGM) youth are more susceptible to suicidal ideation and attempts compared to their heterosexual and cisgender peers. Yet, it is unclear how interpersonal and online victimization experiences account for the elevated suicide risks in this population. This study investigates the extent of peer and cyber victimization among SGM youth and its contribution to their higher risks of suicidal ideation and attempts longitudinally. METHODS: Data were from the first three waves of the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study (5,596 9-10-year-old youth; 2,640 [47.2%] female; 3,107 [55.5%] non-Hispanic White). Youth reported sexual and gender identities and experiences of peer (overt, relational, reputational) and cyber victimization. Suicidal ideation and attempts were assessed using youth reports of Kiddie Schedule for Affective Disorders and Schizophrenia for DSM-5. We used mixed-effects logistic regression to quantify the association between SGM identity reported at waves 1-2 (9-11âyears) and suicidal ideation and attempts at wave 3 (11-12âyears) and longitudinal mediation analysis to determine whether peer and cyber victimization accounted for these associations. RESULTS: SGM youth were at greater risk for lifetime suicidal ideation (odds ratio [OR] 4.75, 95% CI 3.74-6.03), lifetime suicide attempts (OR 5.87, 95% CI 3.72-9.28), and current suicidal ideation or attempts (OR 4.94, 95% CI 3.19-7.68) compared to non-SGM youth. SGM youth experienced elevated peer (overt: Î²â=â0.40, 95% CI 0.31-0.49; relational: Î²â=â0.43, 95% CI 0.34-0.53; reputational: Î²â=â0.51, 95% CI 0.42-0.60) and cyber victimization (OR 2.35, 95% CI 1.77-3.11). Around 22%-28% of the disparities in current suicidal ideation or attempts were mediated by peer or cyber victimization. CONCLUSIONS: SGM youth are disproportionately affected by both interpersonal and online victimization, which are subsequently associated with their elevated suicide risks. Our findings underscore the urgent need for targeted interventions to foster safer school and online environments to reduce suicide among SGM youth.

FT_IC1.evidence: Abstract: 'Data were from the first three waves of the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study (5,596 9-10-year-old youth)'; explicitly uses U.S. ABCD Study data.

FT_IC2.evidence: Paper measures cyber victimization as specific online/social harm variable: 'experiences of peer (overt, relational, reputational) and cyber victimization' reported separately. Cyber victimization qualifies as online social behavior exposure.

FT_IC3.evidence: Mixed-effects logistic regression with inferential statistics: 'SGM youth experienced elevated...cyber victimization (OR 2.35, 95% CI 1.77-3.11)' and 'Around 22%-28% of the disparities in current suicidal ideation or attempts were mediated by peer or cyber victimization.' Cyber victimization linked to suicidal outcomes (non-SM outcome).

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric values reported: OR 2.35 (95% CI 1.77-3.11) for cyber victimization; mediation 22%-28%; multiple ORs for suicidal outcomes (4.75, 5.87, 4.94 with CIs).

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: longitudinal mediation study using first three waves of ABCD; reports cyber victimization as mediator of SGM identity → suicidal ideation/attempts.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article (Journal of Child Psychology and Psychiatry, 2025); no evidence of duplication.

C1.FT_EC7_reason: Cyber victimization is a predictor/mediator of suicidal outcomes (non-SM outcome variable), not the dependent variable. Paper includes individual psychological predictor (SGM identity) and downstream outcome (suicidal ideation/attempts). FT-EC7 does not apply.

FT_IC1.evidence: Abstract states: 'Data were from the first three waves of the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study (5,596 9-10-year-old youth...).' Uses U.S. ABCD Study data.

FT_IC2.evidence: Abstract mentions 'experiences of peer (overt, relational, reputational) and cyber victimization' as measured variables. However, cyber victimization measured in isolation with no separately measured smartphone/SM variable. Abstract provides no evidence of a SPECIFIC smartphone or social media variable (e.g., SM time, app use, platform-specific SM, phone ownership). Cyberbullying/victimization alone without separately measured phone/SM fails FT-IC2 per edge case E6.

FT_IC3.evidence: Abstract reports mixed-effects logistic regression: 'SGM youth were at greater risk for lifetime suicidal ideation (odds ratio [OR] 4.75, 95% CI 3.74-6.03)' and 'SGM youth experienced elevated peer (overt: β = 0.40, 95% CI 0.31-0.49; relational: β = 0.43, 95% CI 0.34-0.53; reputational: β = 0.51, 95% CI 0.42-0.60) and cyber victimization (OR 2.35, 95% CI 1.77-3.11).' Reports inferential tests with coefficients.

FT_IC4.evidence: Abstract provides multiple extractable numerics: OR values, β coefficients, 95% CIs, and percentages (22%-28% mediation effect).

FT_IC5.evidence: Longitudinal mediation study with mixed-effects logistic regression examining waves 1-2 predictors (SGM identity) → wave 3 outcomes (suicidality), with mediation through peer and cyber victimization.

FT_IC6.evidence: Journal article published in Journal of Child Psychology and Psychiatry, 2025; no evidence of duplicate/preprint.

C2.FT_EC7_reason: FT-EC7 does NOT apply. Paper does not report smartphone/SM as dependent variable. Instead, it examines SGM identity and victimization (peer and cyber) as predictors of suicidality. Cyber victimization is measured but not a phone/SM variable per se.

title: Neural Sensitivity to Social Media Use: Implications for Sleep Duration in Adolescents.
abstract: Sleep plays a foundational role in adolescent development, supporting emotional regulation, cognition and brain maturation. However, adolescents today face increasing challenges to healthy sleep partly due to widespread social media use and heightened reward sensitivity. While each factor has been studied independently, less is known about how they interact, especially whether brain activation shapes how social media influences sleep. We examined prospective associations between self-reported sleep duration, social media use and brain activation in 1985 adolescents (mean age, Year 2â=â11.91âyears) from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study. Sleep duration was measured via the Munich Chronotype Questionnaire; social media use via the Youth Screen Time Survey and brain activity using fMRI during the Monetary Incentive Delay task. We tested three sets of prospective models to examine how sleep, social media use, executive control and reward-related brain activation influence each other over two years, controlling sociodemographics. Greater social media use at Year 2 predicted shorter sleep duration at Year 4, particularly among adolescents with lower activation in the nucleus accumbens, cingulate gyrus, insula and putamen. Shorter sleep also predicted increased social media use two years later, with effects moderated by activation in the insula and middle frontal gyrus. Longer sleep at Year 2 predicted higher caudate activation at Year 4. Brain activation in reward and executive control-related regions moderated associations between sleep and social media use, suggesting that lower neural engagement may reflect increased susceptibility to the sleep-disrupting effects of social media.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study data (N=1985 adolescents, Year 2 mean age 11.91 years, followed to Year 4)

FT_IC2.evidence: Social media use measured via Youth Screen Time Survey (self-reported)

FT_IC3.evidence: Prospective regression models testing associations between social media use and sleep duration, moderated by brain activation (fMRI during Monetary Incentive Delay task). Multiple tests across sleep→SM and SM→sleep pathways.

FT_IC4.evidence: Abstract states 'Greater social media use at Year 2 predicted shorter sleep duration at Year 4' with effects reported, including moderation by specific brain regions (nucleus accumbens, cingulate gyrus, insula, putamen)

FT_IC5.evidence: Longitudinal empirical analysis examining prospective associations over two years with inferential tests and multiple outcomes

FT_IC6.evidence: Journal article: Journal of Sleep Research, 2026, DOI 10.1111/jsr.70325

C1.FT_EC7_reason: Paper includes smartphone/SM as both IV (predicting sleep) and DV (predicted by sleep), plus includes individual psychological predictors (brain activation in reward/executive control regions), so FT-EC7 does not apply

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study data with 1985 adolescents from Year 2-Year 4

FT_IC2.evidence: Social media use measured via Youth Screen Time Survey; paper explicitly examines social media as exposure variable

FT_IC3.evidence: Prospective models testing associations between social media use and sleep duration, moderated by brain activation (fMRI activity in nucleus accumbens, cingulate gyrus, insula, putamen, caudate, middle frontal gyrus)

FT_IC4.evidence: No PDF available; abstract describes associations qualitatively (e.g., 'Greater social media use at Year 2 predicted shorter sleep duration at Year 4') but does not report numeric coefficients, ORs, p-values, or effect sizes

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical longitudinal ABCD analysis examining prospective associations across two years

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article (Journal of sleep research, 2026); no duplicate evidence

C2.FT_EC7_reason: Paper includes individual psychological/neural predictors (brain activation in reward and executive control regions) moderating associations between sleep and social media use. Per criterion (b), FT-EC7 only applies when predictors are ONLY demographic/contextual; neural features disqualify FT-EC7
